chapters.title: It's time to find your path
chapters.text:
Do you enjoy your job? Be honest; nobody needs to know the answer but you.

Sure, we all have bad days when our to-do lists resemble Mount Everest, and productivity seems like a false prophecy, but do the good days balance it out?

If your job is just a means to an end and you spend your days watching the clock, maybe it's time to try a different route.

This is something Paul Millerd knows only too well. Having spent his early years working hard, dating, and trying to find happiness in NYC, he suddenly realized that none of it made him happy. We're not talking about the "I've had a bad few days" kind of unhappiness, but the existential, "why am I doing this" type — the kind of unhappiness that signals a real need for change. In the end, isn't life too short?

Too many of us follow the default path. We believe we must find a job, make money, buy a house, get married, start a family, then wait for retirement. And why do we do this? Because society tells us that's what we're supposed to do. But what if you could create a new destiny?

Stuck on a path he knew wasn't his, Millerd gave up his job and traveled the world, freelancing as he went. Was it easy? No. Did he doubt himself? Massively. And did he learn more about who he was than if he'd continued on the default path? Certainly.

Stick around if the possibilities nearby inspire you, but you're unsure how to take that all-important first step. The following key points will fire up your imagination, and our final key point will give you actionable tips to finally take the leap toward happiness.

chapters.title: Work to live, or live to work?
chapters.text:
The first thing we learn about work is that we need to do it to earn money and enjoy a good standard of living. There's no arguing that fact, but where did your ideas about work come from, and how have they shifted over the years? You might wonder why this matters, but the first step on the pathless path is digging deep and getting to the root of your inner psyche. After all, you can't find a new way if you don't understand the old one.

For Paul Millerd, a health crisis made him start thinking about his life more deeply. He realized his job no longer brought him joy; he wondered if it ever had. His illness made him understand how misguided his worldview was, but building a new one wasn't an overnight process.

Throughout history, work has been a subject of change and focus. At first, people worked only as much as they needed to maintain their lifestyles, then stopped. Good advice, right? In fact, Aristotle deemed work to be a necessary evil. Instead, he believed in thinking about our place in the universe rather than making money, and people followed his lead for centuries. It wasn't until around the 16th century that an almost obsessional focus on work became the norm.

A growing anxiety set in as people started to focus more on their careers and making money. Am I working hard enough? Am I doing things right? Am I making enough cash? These were all common worries then; ironically, they're still around today.

The educated, hardworking masses are still doing what they're told, but they're no longer getting what they deserve. ~ Seth Godin

Over time, we've somehow made peace with the idea that work should take up most of our time, and we pass this idea down through generations. We work and don't stop to think about it or question it. In many ways, we don't even take a moment to consider whether it's making us happy. Yet, consider the number of hours you spend in your workplace and how many of those hours you feel less than yourself. It makes you think, doesn't it?

A high-flying career is a waste of time if it doesn't fill your life with joy and satisfaction.

chapters.title: Slow and steady wins the race
chapters.text:
Jumping from the default to the pathless path would be a whole lot easier if it were a fast and certain process. Unfortunately, it's far from that, but it's worth it. When Millerd realized he no longer enjoyed his job, it took him a long time to pluck up the courage to leave. In many ways, it was like waking up from a very deep sleep, a gradual awakening that he was definitely not where he was supposed to be.

Whenever Millerd speaks to people about his story, they want to hear about a dramatic change that happened in the blink of an eye. However, in most cases, major transformations happen with several baby steps. Change isn't something that will happen overnight; buckle up and be ready for a real journey that teaches you plenty along the way.

As Millerd started to recover from his health problems, he began to feel unsatisfied and restless. He couldn't quite pinpoint the problem, but a friend summed it up perfectly: choosing to change your life is like having a pebble in your shoe. You can't walk properly; it really bothers you, and you want to stop and shake your shoe to get it out. That's precisely how Millerd felt, and that metaphorical pebble made him pause and take stock of his life.

He realized that the default path didn't give him the space to breathe, and he desperately needed to get out. Once he took the leap and left, he understood that he had burnout, and the thought shocked him.

Burnout happens when you try to do too much and please too many people. Ask yourself: "Who am I doing this for?"

It's also easy to think that quitting your job and trying something new is about courage, but Millerd believes it's about wonder. We stick with things that make us miserable as long as we find plenty of coping mechanisms. Yet, when we open our eyes to the wonder hidden around us, those coping mechanisms are no longer helpful. At that point, the need to change becomes undeniable and deafening.

Learning to exist with this vagueness is vital, especially at the earliest stages of making a change. ~ Paul Millerd

chapters.title: Feel the fear and do it anyway
chapters.text:
One thing always accompanies wandering from the default path — fear. It's unavoidable and, at times, ever-present, yet you must stare it in the face and refuse to give in. The pathless path will force you to face your fears whether you want to or not, but trust that it's a good thing.

An existential crisis can be the key to many locked doors.

Those who dare to venture from the default path think about several common fears. "What if I fail? "What if I'm not good enough? "What if I run out of money?" "What if everyone thinks I'm crazy and leaves me?" And the most common: "What if I regret it?"

These fears, or similar ones, will find you no matter how hard you try to hide, yet reframing them can be useful. Try this:

• Write down the change you're about to make.
• List the worst-case scenarios.
• Identify things you can do to avoid those scenarios.
• Should everything go wrong, list some steps to get back to where you are right now.
• Identify the benefits of attempting the change or getting at least some success.
• Write down the cost of not doing anything and staying where you are.

This exercise involves thinking about the "what ifs" and "maybes," but it keeps everything in perspective. You'll have a clearer view of the possibilities around you and that nothing is ever as bad as you think. For instance, if you worry that you'll make the change and end up broke, you'll see that you can always make money in other ways.

Ultimately, the pathless path cannot be controlled, and it teaches us to go with the flow and see what happens. However, your fears are real, and you must address them. By facing them bravely and unpicking them, you'll quickly see that you can overcome almost any situation with creative thinking.

Having the courage to let go of the life you believe you're supposed to follow will create space for the one that's ultimately meant for you. Millerd credits this process with helping him meet his wife; if he'd never let go of the default path, he wouldn't have met her and wouldn't be as happy as he is now.

Did you know? A process called potentiation amplifies your fear response if you're already scared. In a fearful state, even the smallest thing can seem terrifying.

chapters.title: Stop and smell the roses now
chapters.text:
Retirement seems like years away, doesn't it? Whether you're nearing that magic number or there are decades to go, it can often feel like you're running on a treadmill to the promised land and never quite getting there.

Putting off your happiness until retirement is a huge mistake. What if it never comes?

The concept of retirement was developed in Germany during the late 1800s, and few people took advantage of it at the time. However, people now live longer, so retirement has become more important. The downside is the high expectations of this so-called "golden" time — zero money worries, time, peace, and happiness. So, we work harder to reach this time in our lives before we can finally stop and take a breath. Firstly, for many, retirement isn't the Zen-like time they've dreamed of, and secondly, wouldn't you rather live now than later?

Millerd found great inspiration in "The Four-Hour Workweek," which was made famous by Tim Ferriss. There, Ferriss talks about the idea of mini-retirements. Rather than having your standard vacation every year, take larger breaks throughout your life instead of saving all your "living time" until retirement. Ferriss suggests anything between one to six months, testing out different ways you might like to live. It's a "try before you buy" approach, giving you the space to be creative and perhaps find your new path.

Millerd tried this approach and found it worked exceptionally well for him. His life became full of opportunities and fun, with several "mini-experiments" conducted in various parts of the world. It taught him new ways to live and helped him test out new circumstances. Then, when he found something that worked, he focused on honing the finer details.

The truth is that everyone is trying to reach a perpetual state of success, often for the wrong reasons. We assume that once we reach a milestone (usually predefined on the default path), we'll somehow reach a nirvana-like state of happiness. When that doesn't happen, we feel disappointed and turn our attention to working even harder, assuming we did something wrong. Millerd suggests shifting your mindset from what you don't have to what you can offer. That way, you'll see opportunities rather than failures.

chapters.title: Let your imagination lead the way
chapters.text:
As children, we have no problem letting our imagination flow, dreaming up new worlds and situations we throw ourselves into with abandon. We live in colorful worlds with weird and wonderful creatures, creating new adventures every day. Yet, as we get older, our imagination is suppressed due to the list of tasks we need to complete and the goals we're trying to reach. We don't have time to dream, but have you ever stopped to wonder about the effects of this?

Your imagination is a garden of new ideas waiting to flourish.

All too often, we wait for the best conditions before making a move, but what does that even look like? On the pathless path, you don't wait for things to get better before making a decision; you throw yourself into it with the faith that you'll find a way. It's a constant search for work that brings you joy, helping you find what drives you and gets you out of bed every morning. It's the single best way to be totally who you are, unapologetic and loving every second.

This is where your imagination comes in. There are limitations at every turn on the default path, but the horizon is your only limit on the pathless path. You can dream and experiment, and if it doesn't work? Turn around and try a different direction instead.

It's about playing the long game; life isn't a race, and there is no final destination. A good way to help you find what you're looking for is to work backward. Start with what you don't want rather than thinking about what you want. This process will help you identify potential traps on your journey and how to avoid them. It might seem like negativity, but it's an excellent way to identify problems and gain a new perspective before you even deal with them.

For sure, working with blind faith and following a path with no direction is scary, but don't worry, you're not alone. In our final key point, we'll share some actionable advice to help you navigate your way.

chapters.title: Try this
chapters.text:
Ready to embrace your pathless path? Here's a checklist to help you take the first steps toward a more fulfilling, unconventional journey:
• List your core values: Write down 3 to 5 values that genuinely resonate with who you are, not what others expect of you. Use these to guide your decisions.

• Reframe success: Define what success means to you personally. Include at least three non-material benchmarks that signify a life well-lived.

• Identify your fears: Take 10 minutes to write down what's holding you back. For each fear, brainstorm one small action to address or challenge it.

• Experiment boldly: Choose one small, unconventional step you can try this week — like starting a creative project, reaching out to an inspiring individual, or changing your daily routine.

• Carve out reflection time: Dedicate 10 or 15 minutes daily to reflect on your experiences, emotions, and insights. Track how these align with your desired path.

• Embrace uncertainty: Write a letter to yourself about how uncertainty can be exciting rather than daunting. Keep it visible as a reminder to stay open to possibilities.

• Seek connection: Reach out to one person who embodies a pathless path you admire. Ask them about their journey and any advice they'd share with you.

Remember, your path doesn't need to look like anyone else's. Take one step at a time, trust your instincts, and let your curiosity lead the way. You’ve got this!
